0: <title>CARBON FOOTPRINT ASSESSMENT OF A LARGE-SCALE PIG PRODUCTION SYSTEM IN NORTHERN CHINA: A CASE STUDY </title>
Y. Q. Zhou, H. M. Dong, H. W. Xin, Z. Z. Zhu, W. Q. Huang, Y. Wang

ABSTRACT. China raises 50% of global live pigs. However, few studies on the carbon footprint (CF) of large-scale pig production based on China's actual production conditions have been carried out. In this study, life cycle assessment (LCA) and actual production data of a typical large-scale pig farm in northern China were used to assess the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions or CF associated with the whole process of pig production, including feed production (crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), enteric fermentation, manure management, and energy consumption. The results showed a CF of 3.39 kg CO2-eq per kg of live market pig and relative contributions of 55%, 28%, 13%, and 4% to the total CF by feed production, manure management, farm energy consumption, and enteric fermentation, respectively. Crop planting accounted for 66% of the feed production CF, while feed processing and transportation accounted for the remaining 34%. Long-distance transport of semi-raw feed materials caused by planting-feeding separation and over-fertilization in feed crop planting were two main reasons for the largest contribution of GHG emissions from feed production to the total CF. The CF from nitrogen fertilizer application accounted for 33% to 44% of crop planting and contributed to 16% of the total CF. The CF from the transport of feed ingredients accounted for 17% of the total CF. If the amount of nitrogen fertilizer used for producing the main feed ingredients is reduced from 209 kg hm-2 (for corn) and 216 kg hm-2 (for wheat) to 140 kg hm-2 (corn) and 180 kg hm2 (wheat), the total CF would be reduced by 7%. If the transport distance for feed materials decreased from 325 to 493 km to 30 km, along with reducing the number of empty vehicles for transport, the total CF would be reduced by 18%. The combined CF mitigation potential for over-fertilization and transport distance is 26%. In addition, the use of pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon for manure management can reduce GHG emissions from manure management by 76% as compared to the traditional practice of pit storage and lagoon. This case study reveals the impact of planting-feeding separation and over-fertilization on the CF of the pig supply chain in China. The manure management practice of pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon is much more conductive to reducing the CF as compared to the traditional practice of pit storage and lagoon.
Keywords. Greenhouse gas, Life cycle assessment, Mitigation, Pig.

T he livestock sector accounts for 18% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Steinfeld et al., 2006). Nearly half of global pigs are raised in China (FAOSTAT, 2015). As such, pork production is the main GHG source in China's food supply chain (Xu and Lan, 2016). Therefore, scientific evaluation of the carbon footprint (CF) of China's pig pro-|duction is important for the analysis of emission sources and policy-making on mitigation measures. Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used to build GHG or CF emission assessment models of livestock sectors for different regions and production scales. Examples include the global GLEAM model (MacLeod et al., 2013), the Euro- pean CAPRI model (Lesschen et al., 2011; Weiss and Leip,
Submitted for review in February 2018 as manuscript number PAFS 12805; approved for publication by the Plant, Animal, & Facility Systems Community of ASABE in March 2018.

The authors are Yuanqing Zhou, Doctoral Student, and Hongmin Dong, Professor and Deputy Director, Institute of Environment and Sustainable Development in Agriculture, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Beijing, China; Hongwei Xin, Distinguished Professor, Department of Agricultural and Biosystems Engineering, and Assistant Dean for Research, Iowa State University, Ames, Iowa; Zhiping Zhu, Professor, Institute of Environment and Sustainable Development in Agriculture, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Energy Conservation and Waste Treatment of Agricultural Structures, Ministry of Agriculture, Beijing, China; Wenqiang Huang, Graduate Student, School of Economics, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China; Yue Wang, Assistant Professor, Beijing Academy of Agriculture and Forestry Sciences, Beijing, China. Corresponding author: Hongmin Dong, No. 12 Southern Street of Zhongguancun, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China; phone: +86-10-82109979; e-mail: donghongmin@caas.cn.

2012), the Canadian ULICEES model (Vergé et al., 2016), and the SustainPork model for farms (Noya et al., 2016). On the basis of IPCC (2006) guidelines, the results of these models were presented in different functional units using al- locations of mass, economic value, protein, etc. An adaptive database was fit for each model. For example, parameters for GLEAM were derived from international organizations such as the Statistics Division of the United Nations FAO (FAO- STAT) and United Nations Framework Convention on Cli- mate Change (UNFCCC) (MacLeod et al., 2013). The pa- rameters for CAPRI were derived from European Union Sta- tistics (EUROSTAT), and data on nitrogen excretion were taken from the GAINS database for the EU (Lesschen et al., 2011; Weiss and Leip, 2012). The parameters for ULICEES were derived from Canadian agricultural statistics (Vergé et al., 2016). The calculation process for the SustainPork model
was more detailed and was based on the production parame- ters of farms (Noya et al., 2016).

Based on the above models and databases, CF assessment of pig production has been carried out at different scales and to address different concerns. Basset-Mens and Van der Werf (2005) and Kool et al. (2009) compared the effects of organic and conventional pig production chains on CF. Less- chen et al. (2011) used CAPRI to analyze the CF of EU live- stock products, emphasizing the impact of land use change on CF. Pelletier et al. (2010) focused on comparison of CF based on slat floor-based swine production systems and deep-bedded niche swine production systems. Cederberg (2004) evaluated pork CF in Sweden based on LCA and used economic allocation in a study of animal welfare, environ- mental impacts, and product quality for pig farming. Vergé et al. (2016) and Noya et al. (2016) analyzed the effects of allocation method and functional units on CF assessment of individual farms. These studies provide valuable references for assessing the CF of pig production in China, although they used location-specific databases or parameters in their respective CF assessments. However, considerable differ- ences exist between western countries and China in manure management and crop planting for pig production. These dif- ferences lie in the in-house manure handling practices, out- door manure storage (use of anaerobic digestion or direct land application), the amount of commercial fertilizer versus animal manure used in crop production (more commercial fertilizer tends to be used in China), the geographic separa- tion of locations for feed crop production and swine farms (farther apart in China), and the level of mechanization in crop farming (less mechanization in China). Consequently, the parameters used in the cited studies do not reflect the characteristics of pig production in China, and neither can the results be directly used to support policy-making about GHG mitigation measures for Chinese pig production sys- items.

CF assessment of Chinese pig production systems has just started. Jianyi et al. (2015) and Xu and Lan (2016) used LCA to evaluate the CF of Chinese pork production in the context of CF assessment of Chinese food production. Jianyi et al. (2015) focused on assessing the variations in CF of Chinese food production, including pork, for three decades (1979- 2009). Xu and Lan (2016) compared differences in CF be- tween 22 plant-based foods and six animal-based foods. However, neither study provided details on emissions at dif- ferent stages nor on mitigation measures for pig production, and they lacked detailed analysis of the higher CF caused by excessive fertilizer application and long-distance transport of feedstuffs in China as compared with the EU and North America. Luo et al. (2015) assessed the CF of household and aggregated pig production in Sichuan Province, China, based on a survey of 32 pig farms in Hongya County. How- ever, there was no clear description of the characteristics of feed production or manure management. The results of these studies were also inconsistent with each other. Luo et al. (2015) and Xu and Lan (2016) showed that feed production had the greatest contribution to GHG emissions of pig pro- duction systems in 2012 and 2013, respectively, whereas Jianyi et al. (2015) found that GHG emissions from manure management in 2009 far exceeded emissions from feed pro-

duction in China. With the rapid development of large-scale livestock production in China, where most large-scale pig farms are using industrial feeding systems, there is no owned land for feed crop planting or manure application (Bai et al., 2014). It is therefore necessary to assess GHG emissions based on actual pig production conditions in China, to ana- lyze the sources of GHG emissions from large-scale pig pro- duction systems, and to explore potential mitigation measures.

In this study, a large-scale pig farm in northern China was selected as a typical case for CF assessment. The aim of this study was to quantify the GHG emissions and relative con- tributions of each sector throughout the pig production chain and to explore potential CF mitigation measures. The results of this study are expected to provide scientific data on the CF baseline, potential CF mitigation measures, and policy- making for pig production in China.

Basset-Mens and Van der Werf (2005) and Kool et al. (2009) compared the effects of organic and conventional pig production chains on CF. Less- chen et al. (2011) used CAPRI to analyze the CF of EU live- stock products, emphasizing the impact of land use change on CF. Pelletier et al. (2010) focused on comparison of CF based on slat floor-based swine production systems and deep-bedded niche swine production systems. Cederberg (2004) evaluated pork CF in Sweden based on LCA and used economic allocation in a study of animal welfare, environ- mental impacts, and product quality for pig farming. Vergé et al. (2016) and Noya et al. (2016) analyzed the effects of allocation method and functional units on CF assessment of individual farms. These studies provide valuable references for assessing the CF of pig production in China, although they used location-specific databases or parameters in their respective CF assessments. However, considerable differ- ences exist between western countries and China in manure management and crop planting for pig production. These dif- ferences lie in the in-house manure handling practices, out- door manure storage (use of anaerobic digestion or direct land application), the amount of commercial fertilizer versus animal manure used in crop production (more commercial fertilizer tends to be used in China), the geographic separa- tion of locations for feed crop production and swine farms (farther apart in China), and the level of mechanization in crop farming (less mechanization in China). Consequently, the parameters used in the cited studies do not reflect the characteristics of pig production in China, and neither can the results be directly used to support policy-making about GHG mitigation measures for Chinese pig production sys- items.

CF assessment of Chinese pig production systems has just started. Jianyi et al. (2015) and Xu and Lan (2016) used LCA to evaluate the CF of Chinese pork production in the context of CF assessment of Chinese food production. Jianyi et al. (2015) focused on assessing the variations in CF of Chinese food production, including pork, for three decades (1979- 2009). Xu and Lan (2016) compared differences in CF be- tween 22 plant-based foods and six animal-based foods. However, neither study provided details on emissions at dif- ferent stages nor on mitigation measures for pig production, and they lacked detailed analysis of the higher CF caused by excessive fertilizer application and long-distance transport of feedstuffs in China as compared with the EU and North America. Luo et al. (2015) assessed the CF of household and aggregated pig production in Sichuan Province, China, based on a survey of 32 pig farms in Hongya County. How- ever, there was no clear description of the characteristics of feed production or manure management. The results of these studies were also inconsistent with each other. Luo et al. (2015) and Xu and Lan (2016) showed that feed production had the greatest contribution to GHG emissions of pig pro- duction systems in 2012 and 2013, respectively, whereas Jianyi et al. (2015) found that GHG emissions from manure management in 2009 far exceeded emissions from feed pro-

duction in China. With the rapid development of large-scale livestock production in China, where most large-scale pig farms are using industrial feeding systems, there is no owned land for feed crop planting or manure application (Bai et al., 2014). It is therefore necessary to assess GHG emissions based on actual pig production conditions in China, to ana- lyze the sources of GHG emissions from large-scale pig pro- duction systems, and to explore potential mitigation measures.

In this study, a large-scale pig farm in northern China was selected as a typical case for CF assessment. The aim of this study was to quantify the GHG emissions and relative con- tributions of each sector throughout the pig production chain and to explore potential CF mitigation measures. The results of this study are expected to provide scientific data on the CF baseline, potential CF mitigation measures, and policy- making for pig production in China.
4: <title>CARBON FOOTPRINT ASSESSMENT OF A LARGE-SCALE PIG PRODUCTION SYSTEM IN NORTHERN CHINA: A CASE STUDY </title>
Y. Q. Zhou, H. M. Dong, H. W. Xin, Z. Z. Zhu, W. Q. Huang, Y. Wang

ABSTRACT. China raises 50% of global live pigs. However, few studies on the carbon footprint (CF) of large-scale pig production based on China's actual production conditions have been carried out. In this study, life cycle assessment (LCA) and actual production data of a typical large-scale pig farm in northern China were used to assess the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions or CF associated with the whole process of pig production, including feed production (crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), enteric fermentation, manure management, and energy consumption. The results showed a CF of 3.39 kg CO2-eq per kg of live market pig and relative contributions of 55%, 28%, 13%, and 4% to the total CF by feed production, manure management, farm energy consumption, and enteric fermentation, respectively. Crop planting accounted for 66% of the feed production CF, while feed processing and transportation accounted for the remaining 34%. Long-distance transport of semi-raw feed materials caused by planting-feeding separation and over-fertilization in feed crop planting were two main reasons for the largest contribution of GHG emissions from feed production to the total CF. The CF from nitrogen fertilizer application accounted for 33% to 44% of crop planting and contributed to 16% of the total CF. The CF from the transport of feed ingredients accounted for 17% of the total CF. If the amount of nitrogen fertilizer used for producing the main feed ingredients is reduced from 209 kg hm-2 (for corn) and 216 kg hm-2 (for wheat) to 140 kg hm-2 (corn) and 180 kg hm2 (wheat), the total CF would be reduced by 7%. If the transport distance for feed materials decreased from 325 to 493 km to 30 km, along with reducing the number of empty vehicles for transport, the total CF would be reduced by 18%. The combined CF mitigation potential for over-fertilization and transport distance is 26%. In addition, the use of pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon for manure management can reduce GHG emissions from manure management by 76% as compared to the traditional practice of pit storage and lagoon. This case study reveals the impact of planting-feeding separation and over-fertilization on the CF of the pig supply chain in China. The manure management practice of pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon is much more conductive to reducing the CF as compared to the traditional practice of pit storage and lagoon.
Keywords. Greenhouse gas, Life cycle assessment, Mitigation, Pig.

7: In this study, LCA was used to study a typical large-scale pig farm in Hebei Province, China (37° to 38° N, 115° to 116° E). The annual average temperature at the pig farm lo- cation was 12.4°C in 2015, the year in which this LCA study was based. The main breeds of pigs were Landrace and Large White. The total on-farm swine inventory was 85,210, including 7,200 SOWS, 18,850 nursery pigs, and 59,160 fat- tening pigs. Three types of GHG were assessed: CO2, CH4, and N2O, with respective 100-year horizon global warming potentials of 1, 25, and 298 (IPCC, 2007).
8: The accounting scope of GHG emissions in this study ranged from feed production to farm gate. The system boundary is shown in figure 1 and included a feed production module and an animal production module. The feed produc- tion module consisted of fertilizer production and transpor- tation, agricultural film and pesticide production, energy consumption for irrigation and agricultural machinery, ferti- lizer application, and feed processing and transportation. The animal production module consisted of enteric fermen- tation, manure management, and farm energy consumption.

For the raw feed materials on the farm, corn accounted for 63%, soybean meal 18%, wheat bran 12%, and other components (such as soya oil, fish meal, wheat flour, cal- cium hydrophosphate, salt, glucose, and lysine) about 7%. This study only calculated GHG emissions for the three ma- jor feedstuffs (corn, soybean meal, and wheat bran). Emis- sions from land use change, soil carbon, and infrastructure related to production, such as farm construction and equip- ment manufacturing, are outside the system boundary. Hence, these emissions were excluded in the CF assessment.
9: In this study, 1 kg live weight (LW) was chosen as the functional unit. The choice of different allocation methods has a large impact on the results of CF assessment. ISO 14044 recommendations state that, in principle, allocation should be avoided; if allocation cannot be avoided, system
* Include commercial fertilizer and animal manure
Diesel fuel|Crop production||Crop pretreatment Coal Fertilizer*|Corn||Corn Agricultural film|Soybean||Soybean meal Pesticide|Wheat||Wheat bran
Figure 1. System boundary of pig production in this carbon footprint assessment.

expansion should be chosen preferentially (ISO, 2006). In this study, taking into account the volatility of the market price of feed crop products, mass allocation was used for GHG emission allocation between the main products and by- products of feed crops. The proportion of GHG emissions allocated to corn grain relative to the total corn crop was 60%; this value was 32% for soybean meal and 25% for wheat bran (Bi et al., 2009).

11: The equations for calculating GHG emissions from farm sources are shown in tables la and 1b. The calculation of fuel consumption for transportation was derived from Lin- eng et al. (2003). This study assumes that (1) all the trans- portation uses diesel trucks, (2) a complete transport task in- cludes a vehicle with no load or full load, and (3) a partial journey is treated as a full journey.

Following IPCC (2007) guidelines, published data re- flecting domestic production practices were used, when available, as the parameters for GHG emissions calculation in this study. When not available, data according to expert recommendations or through indirect calculation were used. The details are described below.
12: In this study, data related to animal production in the CF calculation were obtained through on-site survey. The main herd parameters of the pig farm are shown in table 2.
13: The corn, soybean meal, and wheat bran considered in this study were produced in Jinzhong City (Shanxi Prov- ince), Linyi City (Shandong Province), and Shenzhou City

(Hebei Province), respectively. The data required for the GHG emission calculation were acquired from the 2016 Na- tional Data Compilation of Revenue and Cost of Agricultural Products (NDRC, 2016) by referring to and recalculating the statistical data for the corresponding region and correspond- ing year (table 3).

In order to make the calculation results more representa- tive of and suitable for pig production in China, GHG emis- sion factors related to production, transportation, and use of fertilizers, agricultural film, pesticides, agricultural diesel, and coal during feed production were taken from domestic studies (Chen et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). The emission factors of the northern China power grid were chosen to cal- culate emissions form electricity consumption (NDRC, 2013) (table 4).
14: Manure in the gestation, lactating sow/piglet, and nursery houses with slatted floors was collected in the pit and stored for about five days before removed. Manure in the fattening pig houses with solid floors was collected in a gutter outside the pig house and removed daily. There was no solid-liquid separation of the manure. In all cases, pig manure was trans- ported to the on-site biogas plant for biogas production, and the biogas digester effluent was pumped into the lagoon. Subsequently, the lagoon effluent was transported through an underground pipe to the local county wastewater treat- ment center, where it was processed together with other in- dustrial wastewaters and urban sewage. In the calculation of N2O emissions from manure management, the nitrogen ex- cretion (Nex) rate was based on data provided in the First Na- tional Pollution Census Bulletin of China (CNEPA, 2010). The Nex rate for each category of pigs was 33 g N head-1 d-Superscript(1) for fattening pigs, 20 g N head-1 d-Superscript(1) for piglets, and 44 g N head-1 d-Superscript(1) for SOWS. The manure management process was a composite sys- tem due to different durations of manure storage. As such, VS and Nex were consumed in each stage of manure manage- ment. The VS and Nex consumption rates during in-house pit storage were 3% and 11%, respectively (IPCC, 2006; Wang et al., 2017). In comparison, the VS and Nex consumption rates during anaerobic digestion were 70% and 5%, respec- tively (Nasir et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017).

23: The annual GHG emissions from the pig supply chain were estimated to be 53,225 t CO2-eq, and the CF was 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. From a global perspective, the CF per kg pork varies considerably, ranging from 2.3 to 8.7 kg CO2-eq due to differences in system boundary, allocation method, functional unit, manure management, and SO on (table 6). The French good agricultural practice for pig pro- duction was shown to have the smallest CF of 2.3 kg CO2- eq kg ¹ LW (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005). In com- parison, Noya et al. (2016) included GHG emissions from the slaughter and processing stages as well as feed produc- tion and animal production, and they made no distinction in GHG allocation between the main products and by-products of feed crops, which led to the largest CF of 6.09 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW, or an equivalent of 8.7 kg CO2-eq kg-1 CW (CW = carcass weight, LW to CW ratio = 0.7).

Although the CF of pork could not be compared readily among different studies, we attempted to compare our study with studies that had a similar assessment scope of CF (from feed production to farm gate) and converted the functional unit from CW to LW with a CW to LW ratio of 0.7. The CF observed in this study was close to the results of Luo et al. (2015) (3.0 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) and Wiedemann et al. (2016) (3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) but was higher than that of Pelletier et al. (2010), Kool et al. (2009), and González-Gar- cía et al. (2015) (table 6). The following factors may explain the differences in emission intensity between this study and others. First, there were differences in emission sources for the same scope of feed production to farm gate. This study included CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation during animal production, which was not considered by Luo et al. (2015). Lesschen et al. (2011), Weiss and Leip (2012), Pelle- tier et al. (2010), and Kool et al. (2009) only calculated on-

farm direct energy consumption and did not include indirect energy consumption of feed transportation. Second, this study used a mass allocation method for GHG emissions be- tween the main product and by-product of feed crops, yield- ing a 32% distribution ratio of soybean meal. In comparison, Wiedemann et al. (2016) used economic value to allocate GHG emissions of the main product and by-product of feed crops, which yielded a 62% allocation ratio for soybean meal and a slightly higher CF of 3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. Gonzá- lez-García et al. (2015) used a system expansion method on the pork supply chain to avoid repeated calculation of GHG emissions from the system modules. The result was a CF of 2.6 kg CO2-eq kg LW, which is significantly lower than the result of our study. Furthermore, feed conversion ratio (FCR) is a key factor that influences CF assessment. In this study, the FCR of growing-finishing pigs was 2.68. How- ever, it was 2.44 in Pelletier et al. (2010), who reported a CF

of kg CO2-eq LW, which is half the magnitude found in our study. The FCR values reported by Wiedemann et al. (2016) and Weiss and Leip (2012) were 3.1 and 4.1, respec- tively, and the corresponding CF values (3.6 and 5.2 kg CO2- eq kg-1 LW) were larger than that of our study. Other param- eters used in the calculation could also influence the results. For instance, the emission factor for electric power in north- ern China was 0.8843 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 versus 0.92 kg CO2- eq kWh-1 in Luo et al. (2015), which was higher than the national average. In this particular case, the use of 0.8843 versus 0.92 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 led to a CF difference of 4%.

(2016) and Weiss and Leip (2012) were 3.1 and 4.1, respec- tively, and the corresponding CF values (3.6 and 5.2 kg CO2- eq kg-1 LW) were larger than that of our study. Other param- eters used in the calculation could also influence the results. For instance, the emission factor for electric power in north- ern China was 0.8843 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 versus 0.92 kg CO2- eq kWh-1 in Luo et al. (2015), which was higher than the national average. In this particular case, the use of 0.8843 versus 0.92 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 led to a CF difference of 4%.
25: The annual GHG emissions from the pig supply chain were estimated to be 53,225 t CO2-eq, and the CF was 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. From a global perspective, the CF per kg pork varies considerably, ranging from 2.3 to 8.7 kg CO2-eq due to differences in system boundary, allocation method, functional unit, manure management, and SO on (table 6). The French good agricultural practice for pig pro- duction was shown to have the smallest CF of 2.3 kg CO2- eq kg ¹ LW (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005). In com- parison, Noya et al. (2016) included GHG emissions from the slaughter and processing stages as well as feed produc- tion and animal production, and they made no distinction in GHG allocation between the main products and by-products of feed crops, which led to the largest CF of 6.09 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW, or an equivalent of 8.7 kg CO2-eq kg-1 CW (CW = carcass weight, LW to CW ratio = 0.7).

Although the CF of pork could not be compared readily among different studies, we attempted to compare our study with studies that had a similar assessment scope of CF (from feed production to farm gate) and converted the functional unit from CW to LW with a CW to LW ratio of 0.7. The CF observed in this study was close to the results of Luo et al. (2015) (3.0 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) and Wiedemann et al. (2016) (3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) but was higher than that of Pelletier et al. (2010), Kool et al. (2009), and González-Gar- cía et al. (2015) (table 6). The following factors may explain the differences in emission intensity between this study and others. First, there were differences in emission sources for the same scope of feed production to farm gate. This study included CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation during animal production, which was not considered by Luo et al. (2015). Lesschen et al. (2011), Weiss and Leip (2012), Pelle- tier et al. (2010), and Kool et al. (2009) only calculated on-

farm direct energy consumption and did not include indirect energy consumption of feed transportation. Second, this study used a mass allocation method for GHG emissions be- tween the main product and by-product of feed crops, yield- ing a 32% distribution ratio of soybean meal. In comparison, Wiedemann et al. (2016) used economic value to allocate GHG emissions of the main product and by-product of feed crops, which yielded a 62% allocation ratio for soybean meal and a slightly higher CF of 3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. Gonzá- lez-García et al. (2015) used a system expansion method on the pork supply chain to avoid repeated calculation of GHG emissions from the system modules. The result was a CF of 2.6 kg CO2-eq kg LW, which is significantly lower than the result of our study. Furthermore, feed conversion ratio (FCR) is a key factor that influences CF assessment. In this study, the FCR of growing-finishing pigs was 2.68. How- ever, it was 2.44 in Pelletier et al. (2010), who reported a CF

For instance, the emission factor for electric power in north- ern China was 0.8843 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 versus 0.92 kg CO2- eq kWh-1 in Luo et al. (2015), which was higher than the national average. In this particular case, the use of 0.8843 versus 0.92 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 led to a CF difference of 4%.
28: The annual GHG emissions from the pig supply chain were estimated to be 53,225 t CO2-eq, and the CF was 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. From a global perspective, the CF per kg pork varies considerably, ranging from 2.3 to 8.7 kg CO2-eq due to differences in system boundary, allocation method, functional unit, manure management, and SO on (table 6). The French good agricultural practice for pig pro- duction was shown to have the smallest CF of 2.3 kg CO2- eq kg ¹ LW (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005). In com- parison, Noya et al. (2016) included GHG emissions from the slaughter and processing stages as well as feed produc- tion and animal production, and they made no distinction in GHG allocation between the main products and by-products of feed crops, which led to the largest CF of 6.09 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW, or an equivalent of 8.7 kg CO2-eq kg-1 CW (CW = carcass weight, LW to CW ratio = 0.7).

Although the CF of pork could not be compared readily among different studies, we attempted to compare our study with studies that had a similar assessment scope of CF (from feed production to farm gate) and converted the functional unit from CW to LW with a CW to LW ratio of 0.7. The CF observed in this study was close to the results of Luo et al. (2015) (3.0 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) and Wiedemann et al. (2016) (3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) but was higher than that of Pelletier et al. (2010), Kool et al. (2009), and González-Gar- cía et al. (2015) (table 6). The following factors may explain the differences in emission intensity between this study and others. First, there were differences in emission sources for the same scope of feed production to farm gate. This study included CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation during animal production, which was not considered by Luo et al. (2015). Lesschen et al. (2011), Weiss and Leip (2012), Pelle- tier et al. (2010), and Kool et al. (2009) only calculated on-

farm direct energy consumption and did not include indirect energy consumption of feed transportation. Second, this study used a mass allocation method for GHG emissions be- tween the main product and by-product of feed crops, yield- ing a 32% distribution ratio of soybean meal. In comparison, Wiedemann et al. (2016) used economic value to allocate GHG emissions of the main product and by-product of feed crops, which yielded a 62% allocation ratio for soybean meal and a slightly higher CF of 3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. Gonzá- lez-García et al. (2015) used a system expansion method on the pork supply chain to avoid repeated calculation of GHG emissions from the system modules. The result was a CF of 2.6 kg CO2-eq kg LW, which is significantly lower than the result of our study. Furthermore, feed conversion ratio (FCR) is a key factor that influences CF assessment. In this study, the FCR of growing-finishing pigs was 2.68. How- ever, it was 2.44 in Pelletier et al. (2010), who reported a CF

In this particular case, the use of 0.8843 versus 0.92 kg CO2-eq kWh-1 led to a CF difference of 4%.
31: The annual GHG emissions from the pig supply chain were estimated to be 53,225 t CO2-eq, and the CF was 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. From a global perspective, the CF per kg pork varies considerably, ranging from 2.3 to 8.7 kg CO2-eq due to differences in system boundary, allocation method, functional unit, manure management, and SO on (table 6). The French good agricultural practice for pig pro- duction was shown to have the smallest CF of 2.3 kg CO2- eq kg ¹ LW (Basset-Mens and Van der Werf, 2005). In com- parison, Noya et al. (2016) included GHG emissions from the slaughter and processing stages as well as feed produc- tion and animal production, and they made no distinction in GHG allocation between the main products and by-products of feed crops, which led to the largest CF of 6.09 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW, or an equivalent of 8.7 kg CO2-eq kg-1 CW (CW = carcass weight, LW to CW ratio = 0.7).

Although the CF of pork could not be compared readily among different studies, we attempted to compare our study with studies that had a similar assessment scope of CF (from feed production to farm gate) and converted the functional unit from CW to LW with a CW to LW ratio of 0.7. The CF observed in this study was close to the results of Luo et al. (2015) (3.0 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) and Wiedemann et al. (2016) (3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW) but was higher than that of Pelletier et al. (2010), Kool et al. (2009), and González-Gar- cía et al. (2015) (table 6). The following factors may explain the differences in emission intensity between this study and others. First, there were differences in emission sources for the same scope of feed production to farm gate. This study included CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation during animal production, which was not considered by Luo et al. (2015). Lesschen et al. (2011), Weiss and Leip (2012), Pelle- tier et al. (2010), and Kool et al. (2009) only calculated on-

farm direct energy consumption and did not include indirect energy consumption of feed transportation. Second, this study used a mass allocation method for GHG emissions be- tween the main product and by-product of feed crops, yield- ing a 32% distribution ratio of soybean meal. In comparison, Wiedemann et al. (2016) used economic value to allocate GHG emissions of the main product and by-product of feed crops, which yielded a 62% allocation ratio for soybean meal and a slightly higher CF of 3.6 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. Gonzá- lez-García et al. (2015) used a system expansion method on the pork supply chain to avoid repeated calculation of GHG emissions from the system modules. The result was a CF of 2.6 kg CO2-eq kg LW, which is significantly lower than the result of our study. Furthermore, feed conversion ratio (FCR) is a key factor that influences CF assessment. In this study, the FCR of growing-finishing pigs was 2.68. How- ever, it was 2.44 in Pelletier et al. (2010), who reported a CF

33: <header>CONTRIBUTIONS AND POTENTIAL MITIGATION OF DIFFERENT PRODUCTION STAGES </header>

The results showed that the CF per kg of live market pig was 3.39 kg CO2-eq, and the contributions of feed produc- tion, enteric fermentation, manure management, and farm energy consumption to CF were 55%, 4%, 28%, and 13%, respectively. According to the previously documented stud- ies, the contribution of feed production varied from 49% for North America (MacLeod et al., 2013) to 83% for aggre- gated pig production in Sichuan Province, China (Luo et al., 2015). Jianyi et al. (2015) also found that the relative contri- bution of feed production to CF per kg of pork decreased from 50.9% in 1979 to 33.2% in 2009.

Manure management is the second largest emission source after feed production, accounting for 28% of the total CF. The relative contribution of manure management varied from 12% to 41% in the documented studies. Luo et al. (2015) found that manure management contributed only 12% of the total GHG emissions from the production system when assessing the CF of the pork supply chain in Sichuan Province, China. Lesschen et al. (2011) found that manure
management contributed 41% of the total CF in an assess- ment of the EU-27 pork supply chain.

35: GHG emissions from feed production are mainly derived from feed crop planting, feed processing, and feed transpor- tation. The emissions from crop planting accounted for 66% of the CF of feed production, whereas feed processing and transportation contributed the remaining 34%.
36: Because of different planting measures, the CF magni- tude and partitioning for different feed materials were also different. The CF of corn, soybean meal, and wheat bran was 0.51, 0.12, and 0.53 kg CO2-eq kg respectively. As shown by the data in figure 2, emissions from nitrogen fertilizer ap- plication accounted for the majority of the total CF, i.e., 44%, 41%, and 33% for corn, soybean meal, and wheat bran, respectively. The lower contribution of nitrogen fertilizer ap- plication for wheat bran production resulted from the larger amount of water used in wheat irrigation. Compared with the global average of 28% for the relative contribution of nitro- gen fertilizer application for feed production (MacLeod et al., 2013), the higher value in this study was mainly due to the larger nitrogen fertilizer input for Chinese crops (Zhang et al., 2013).

An important way to reduce the CF of crops is by reduc- ing nitrogen fertilizer application rationally based on crop nutrient requirements (Jianyi et al., 2015; Ju et al., 2009). In this study, the amounts of nitrogen fertilizer applied in corn and wheat production were 209 and 216 kg hm-² respec- tively, which were significantly higher than the amount ap- plied for corn in North America (152 kg N hm-2 (Grassini and Cassman, 2012). The European Union's Good Agricul-

||||Feed pretreatment ||||Fertilizer transportation ||||Fertilizer production ||||Nitrogen application ||||Agricultural film & pesticides ||||Irrigation ||||N Agricultural machinery Corn|Corn|Soybean meal|Wheat bran|
Figure 2. Partitioning of carbon footprint of feed materials by production component.

tural Practice for Protection of Waters recommends maxi- mum N fertilization rates for corn and wheat of 75 to 180 kg hm²² and 80 to 210 kg hm-2. respectively (EU, 2014). Assum- ing that nitrogen fertilizer application for corn and wheat is reduced to the EU-recommended class II values of 140 kg hm-2 for corn and 180 kg hm-2 for wheat, the GHG emissions from nitrogen fertilizer applications for corn and wheat will be reduced by 33% (from 7,652 to 5,136 t CO2-eq) and 17% (from 1,141 to 951 t CO2-eq), respectively. The proportion of emissions from nitrogen fertilizer applications for corn and wheat to the total emissions from crop planting will be decreased by 7% (from 44% to 37%) and 3% (from 33% to 30%), respectively. These reductions translate to 3,628 t CO2-eq lower annual GHG emissions. Accordingly, the CF per kg of live market pig would decrease from 3.39 to 3.16 kg CO2-eq, a reduction of nearly 7%.

39: The feed transport distance directly affects the CF of feed production and animal production. In this study, emissions from long-distance transport (300 to 500 km) of raw feed materials contributed 31% of the feed production CF and 17% of the total CF because the corn and soybean meal were

sourced from other provinces. Pork CF assessment of house- hold and aggregated farms in Sichuan Province, China, showed a feed transportation CF of 0.01 kg CO2-eq kg LW (Luo et al., 2015), which is much lower than the 0.57 kg CO2-eq kg- 1 LW obtained in our study. Noya et al. (2016) found that feed transport accounted for 17% of total GHG emissions from feed production due to the large distances between feed growing areas and feed processing plants in the Spanish pig production chain. A similar result (4% to 17%) was reported for the Portuguese pork supply chain (Gonzá- lez-García et al., 2015).

Shortening the transport distance and reducing the num- ber of no-load transport vehicles are important ways to re- duce the CF of pig production. In this study, the transport distances of corn and soybean meal between the feed grow- ing area and the swine farm were 325 and 495 km, respec- tively, and each transport of raw feed materials involved an empty vehicle traveling to the feed growing area, resulting in an annual GHG emission of 9,989 t CO2-eq for feed trans- portation. Using one-way transport distances of 325 km for soybean meal and 495 km for corn with full loads (fig. 3b), the GHG emissions from feed transportation will be reduced

Figure 3. Comparison of carbon footprints from feed production for four feed transportation scenarios: (a) two-way load (one way at full load and one way empty) with transport distances of 650 km for corn and 990 km for soybean meal, (b) one way at full load with transport distances of 325 km for corn and 495 km for soybean meal, (c) two-way load (one way at full load and one way empty) with a transport distance of 60 km for corn and soybean meal, and (d) one-way load with a transport distance of 30 km for corn and soybean meal.

to 5,957 CO2-eq. The resulting contribution to the feed pro- duction CF of this emission source will be reduced from 31% to 18%, and the CF of per kg live market pig will be reduced by 9% to 3.07 kg CO2-eq. Using a two-way transport dis- tance of 30 km with one-way loads for corn and soybean meal (fig. 3c), the GHG emissions from feed transport will be reduced to 921 t CO2-eq, and the relative contribution to feed production CF will be reduced to 4%. The correspond- ing CF of pork will be reduced by 17% to 2.81 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. Finally, using a one-way load with a transport dis- tance of 30 km for corn and soybean meal (fig. 3d), the GHG emissions from feed transport will be reduced to 549 t CO2- eq, and the relative contribution to feed production CF will be reduced to only 2% of the total CF. The overall CF will be reduced to 2.78 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW, which is 18% lower than the baseline scenario. Thus, obtaining feedstocks from regions within 30 km of the pig farm and avoiding empty vehicles could reduce the total CF of the pig supply chain by 9% to 18%.

43: Manure management included three storage stages, i.e., in-house storage, outdoor storage, and manure application on the farmland. Because the GHG emissions from manure application during feed crop planting has already been in- cluded in the CF calculation, the emissions from manure ap- plication on farmland were not included to avoid double counting. The CF of the manure management stage was 1.04 kg CO2-eq kg LW, and the CF for in-house emissions and outdoor manure treatment accounted for 16% and 84%, respectively, of the manure management CF (fig. 4).
44: Emissions from in-house manure management included CH4 and N2O emissions from the manure storage. The GHG emitted from the animal house contributed only 4% of the
Figure 4. Carbon footprint of manure management (CH4 emission from anaerobic digestion results from leakage of biogas digestion system).

total CF, at 0.16 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW (fig. 4). The main rea- son for the smaller value is the short-term storage. The ma- nure from SOWS and nursery pigs was collected in pits and stored for five days, whereas the manure from fattening pigs was collected and removed daily.

The N2O emissions from manure management are closely related to the Nex rate of the pigs. A low crude protein diet plays an important role in reducing the Nex of pigs and N- related gaseous emissions of manure management (Wang et al. 2017). In this study, the value of Nex used to calculate N2O emissions from manure management was based on the emis- sion factors published in China. The Nex rates for fattening pigs, nursery pigs, and SOWS were 33, 20, and 44 g N head-1 d-1, respectively. Compared with the IPCC default values of Nex (29 g N head-Superscript(1) d-Superscript(1) for fattening pigs, 11 g N head-1 d-Superscript(1) for nursery pigs, and 51 g N head-1 d-Superscript(1) for sows), the Nex values in our study were 12% and 45% higher for fattening pigs and nursery pigs, respectively, but 15% lower for SOWS. Osada et al. (2011) and Ogino et al. (2013) showed that if the crude protein content in the animal diet was reduced to 85%, the Nex would decrease by more than 20%. Low-protein diets re- duce the use of soy-based feedstuffs while slightly increas- ing the use of cereals or synthetic amino acids. However, the GHG emissions from feed production showed a downward trend. If the Nex values for fattening and nursery pigs in our study are reduced to the IPCC default values by reducing the dietary crude protein content, the proportion of emissions re- lated to Nex in manure management will be reduced by 15%.

46: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 47: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 48: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 49: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because
Table 7. Comparison of GHG emissions (t CO2-eq year-1) from two types of manure management.

Source|Scenario A (pit storage and lagoon)|Scenario B (pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon)|Reduction by Scenario B Pit storage|2,617|2,617|0% Anaerobic digestion|-|6,638|N/A Lagoon|43,300|7,110|84% Power consumption of biogas engineering||654|N/A Methane generation|-|-5,845|N/A Total|45,918|11,174|76%

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 50: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

Source|Scenario A (pit storage and lagoon)|Scenario B (pit storage, anaerobic digestion, and lagoon)|Reduction by Scenario B Pit storage|2,617|2,617|0% Anaerobic digestion|-|6,638|N/A Lagoon|43,300|7,110|84% Power consumption of biogas engineering||654|N/A Methane generation|-|-5,845|N/A Total|45,918|11,174|76%

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 51: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 52: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 53: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 54: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 55: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU.
