CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 56: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%. 57: Emissions from outdoor manure storage contributed 24% of the total CF, at 0.42 kg CO2-eq kg- LW for anaerobic digestion and 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW for the lagoon (fig. 4). In this study, manure was collected first for use in anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, and then CH4 was recovered and used for biogas generation. In order to explore mitiga- tion potentials for manure management, GHG emissions from the traditional manure treatment practice of pit storage and lagoon (baseline scenario A) were compared with the manure management practice (scenario B) in this study (table 7).

Table 7 shows that the GHG emissions from manure man- agement in scenario A totaled 45,918 t CO2-eq year Emis- sions from the lagoon accounted for 94% of the total. The CF of the pig production system was 5.27 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW with traditional manure treatment. In scenario B, emis- sions from manure management were reduced by 34,744 t CO2-eq year because of the anaerobic digestion before la- goon storage. The GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were reduced by 76% after anaerobic fermentation. Compared with manure without anaerobic treatment, the CH4 emissions from manure were reduced by 75% because

<table>of anaerobic digestion and recycling of biogas. This is lower than the modeled CH4 reduction of 90% reported by Sommer et al. (2004). The digested manure can increase the amount of direct nitrogen available to plants and reduce the synthetic fertilizer requirement, which can reduce the CF of feed pro- duction (Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009; Weiland, 2010). As an effective CF mitigation measure, anaerobic digestion is one of the recommended manure management practices in China, as stated in the Thirteenth Five-Year Biogas Devel- opment Plan jointly issued by the China National Develop- ment and Reform Commission and the China Ministry of Agriculture. The total investment in rural biogas projects will be 50 billion yuan (RMB) during the period of the Thir- teenth Five-Year National Strategic Plan. The number of bi- ogas projects in China was 113,182 in 2015 (China Agricul- ture, 2016). Upflow anaerobic sludge bed (UASB) reactors, continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) and upflow solid reactors (USRs) are common types of anaerobic digesters for animal manure in China. The produced biogas is used as fuel for heating and/or electric power generation, with the gener- ated electricity integrated into the national electric power grid.|"de Dalgaard, CNEPA. China EU. CONCLUSION This case study used LCA to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of a large-scale pig production chain based on actual production conditions in northern China. It also identified potential areas for mitigating the CF. The results showed that the CF of a large-scale pig production chain in northern China is 3.39 kg CO2-eq kg-1 LW. The relative contributions to the total CF were 55% for feed production (including crop planting, feed processing, and transportation), 28% for ma- nure management, 13% for farm energy consumption, and 4% for enteric fermentation. Nitrogen fertilizer application and feedstuff transportation were key sources of GHG emis- sions for feed production. Compared to traditional manure management practices (pit storage and lagoon), anaerobic di- gestion of manure and recycling of CH4, as used in this case study, can reduce the CF of the pig supply chain by 76%.
